statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Two arrays $ p $ and $ s $ of length $ n $ are given, where $ p $ is the prefix GCD $ ^{\text{РѕЌ}} $ of some array $ a $ , and $ s $ is the suffix GCD of the same array $ a $ . In other words, if the array $ a $ existed, then for each $ 1 \le i \le n $ , the following equalities would hold both:

- $ p_i = \gcd(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_i) $
- $ s_i = \gcd(a_i, a_{i+1}, \dots, a_n) $ .

Determine whether there exists such an array $ a $ for which the given arrays $ p $ and $ s $ can be obtained. $ ^{\text{РѕЌ}} $ $ \gcd(x, y) $ denotes the [greatest common divisor (GCD)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greatest_common_divisor) of integers $ x $ and $ y $ .

Input Format
The first line contains an integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ) Рђћ the number of test cases.

Each test case consists of three lines:

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \leq n \leq 10^5 $ ) Рђћ the length of the array.

The second line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n $ ( $ 1 \leq p_i \le 10^9 $ ) Рђћ the elements of the array.

The third line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n $ ( $ 1 \leq s_i \le 10^9 $ ) Рђћ the elements of the array.

It is guaranteed that the sum of all $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output "Yes" (without quotes) if there exists an array $ a $ for which the given arrays $ p $ and $ s $ can be obtained, and "No" (without quotes) otherwise.

You may output each letter in any case (lowercase or uppercase). For example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes", and "YES" will be accepted as a positive answer.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
6
72 24 3 3 3 3
3 3 3 6 12 144
3
1 2 3
4 5 6
5
125 125 125 25 25
25 25 25 25 75
4
123 421 282 251
125 1981 239 223
3
124 521 125
125 121 121
Output
YES
NO
YES
NO
NO

Note
For the first test case, a possible array is: \[ $ 72,\ 24,\ 3,\ 6,\ 12,\ 144 $ \].

For the second test case, it can be shown that such arrays do not exist.

For the third test case, there exists an array: \[ $ 125,\ 125,\ 125,\ 25,\ 75 $ \].

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2126E - лЮ-лъ-лћ, лйлх л┐лЙл▓лхлил╗лЙ!
Problem. We are given two integer arrays $$$p[1 Рђд n]$$$ and $$$s[1 Рђд n]$$$. They are alleged to be the prefix-GCDs and suffix-GCDs of some hidden array $$$a[1 Рђд n]$$$:
$$$p_i$$$ = $$$gcd(a_1,...,a_i)$$$, $$$s_i$$$ = $$$gcd(a_i,...,a_n)$$$.
Decide whether such an array a exists.
StepРђ»1. Immediate divisibility checks. For any prefix GCD chain one always has $$$p_i$$$Рђ»|Рђ»$$$p_{i - 1}$$$ (moving right never introduces new prime factors). Likewise, for the suffix chain $$$s_iРђ»|Рђ»s_{i+1}$$$. So we must have
$$$p_{i-1}$$$ mod $$$p_i = 0$$$ for every $$$iРђ»РЅЦРђ»2$$$, $$$s_{i+1}$$$ mod $$$s_i = 0$$$ for every $$$iРђ»РЅцРђ»nРђ»РѕњРђ»1$$$.
Both chains describe the GCD of the entire array when they meet in the middle, therefore $$$p_n$$$ must equal $$$s_1$$$. Denote that common value by $$$g$$$. If any of these conditions fails, the answer is immediately "NO".
StepРђ»2. Factor $$$g$$$ out. Write $$$P_i = \frac {p_i} {g} $$$ and $$$S_i = \frac {s_i} {g}$$$. From now on we only reason about the reduced chains $$$P$$$ and $$$S$$$; $$$g$$$ can be multiplied back into every $$$a_i$$$ at the end.
StepРђ»3. What must hold at a single position $$$i$$$. Suppose we already know $$$gcd(a_1,Рђд,a_{i-1}) = p_{i-1}$$$ and $$$gcd(a_{i+1},Рђд,a_n) = s_{i+1}$$$. We must choose a positive integer $$$A$$$ such that
$$$gcd(p_{i-1}, A) = p_i$$$ and $$$gcd(A, s_{i+1}) = s_i$$$.
Divide by g everywhere and let
$$$x = \frac {p_{i-1}} {p_i}$$$ (an integer $$$РЅЦРђ»1$$$), $$$y = \frac {s_{i+1}} {s_i} $$$ (also $$$РЅЦРђ»1$$$).
The two equations become
$$$gcd(x, \frac {A} {g}) = 1$$$ and $$$gcd(\frac {A}{g}, y) = 1$$$, $$$P_i$$$ divides $$$\frac {A}{g}$$$, $$$S_i$$$ divides $$$\frac {A} {g}$$$.
Set $$$\frac {A}{g} = u$$$. We need a $$$u$$$ that is a common multiple of $$$P_i$$$ and $$$S_i$$$ and is simultaneously coprime to both $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$.
Key arithmetic facts: $$$P_i$$$ and $$$x$$$ are always coprime, because $$$x = \frac {P_{i-1}} {P_i}$$$ and no prime can divide both a number and its own quotient. Symmetrically, $$$S_i$$$ and $$$y$$$ are coprime.
Therefore $$$u = P_iРђ»*Рђ»S_i$$$ works iff
$$$gcd(x, S_i) = 1$$$ and $$$gcd(y, P_i) = 1$$$.
These two gcd tests are both necessary and sufficient; if they pass we can put $$$a_i = gРђ»*Рђ»P_iРђ»*Рђ»S_i$$$.
StepРђ»4. Global algorithm.
1. Verify the divisibility chains and $$$p_n = s_1$$$; if something fails, print NO.
2. For every $$$i = 2Рђдn$$$ check $$$gcd((\frac {p_{i-1}}{p_i}), S_i) = 1$$$.
3. For every $$$i = n-1Рђд1$$$ check $$$gcd((\frac {s_{i+1}}{s_i}), P_i) = 1$$$. (All divisions are exact by the chain property.)
4. If every test passes, print YES; otherwise NO.
StepРђ»5. Complexity. Each gcd call is logarithmic in the values involved; we make $$$O(n)$$$ such calls and a constant number of simple passes over the arrays, so the total running time per test case is $$$O(nРђ»logРђ»maxvalue)$$$ with $$$O(1)$$$ extra memory.
If the algorithm answers YES, one explicit witness array is $$$a_i = gРђ»*Рђ»P_iРђ»*Рђ»S_i$$$.
